cases.title: Diagnose a remote cluster-share failure
cases.student_prompt: A worker can SSH to the scheduler but AGILAB post-install still fails. Identify the weak assumption, the evidence-backed root cause, the best fix, and the regression checks.
cases.symptom: Remote workers can SSH to the scheduler but fail during post-install because the configured cluster share is not writable on the worker.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The worker cannot reach the scheduler over SSH.
cases.root_cause: The original diagnosis confuses SSH reachability with shared-storage availability; the worker needs the scheduler-side AGI_CLUSTER_SHARE mounted through SSHFS at the worker data path.
cases.plain_repro: ssh worker 'test -w ~/clustershare/agi && python -m agi_node.agi_dispatcher.post_install flight_worker'
weak_assumptions: SSH login success proves the shared data path is usable.
Scheduler and worker paths must be identical across operating systems.
The install error is an app dependency issue rather than a cluster-share contract issue.

evidence.description: Non-interactive SSH to the worker succeeds.
post_install fails while resolving AGI_CLUSTER_SHARE on the worker.
The scheduler uses a macOS path and the worker uses a Linux home path.

candidate_fixes.summary: Keep scheduler AGI_CLUSTER_SHARE as source and mount it on workers with SSHFS before post-install.
Require all workers to use the scheduler absolute path.

regression_tests.description: Run share-check-only doctor with scheduler and remote worker paths.
Run worker post-install after the SSHFS mount is prepared.
Simulate an existing stale mount and verify remount logic.

student_answer.diagnosis: The initial diagnosis stops too early at SSH reachability.
student_answer.root_cause: The original diagnosis confuses SSH reachability with shared-storage availability; the worker needs the scheduler-side AGI_CLUSTER_SHARE mounted through SSHFS at the worker data path.

cases.title: Explain a stale WORKFLOW DAG preview
cases.student_prompt: A DAG preview shows old completed stages after lab_stages.toml changed. Explain why the Graphviz diagnosis is weak and choose a regression-backed fix.
cases.symptom: The WORKFLOW DAG preview shows completed project stages after lab_stages.toml has been edited.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The DAG renderer is caching Graphviz output.
cases.root_cause: The runner-state preview did not compare the project-step digest or latest run-log timestamp before reusing previous execution evidence.
cases.plain_repro: Run a workflow, edit lab_stages.toml, reopen WORKFLOW and inspect runner_state.json.
weak_assumptions: A visual rendering issue explains stale execution status.
A successful previous run remains valid after the project step contract changes.

evidence.description: The saved runner state contains a digest for the previous lab_stages.toml.
The latest pipeline log is older than the edited lab_stages.toml.

candidate_fixes.summary: Derive DAG status from run logs and mark previews stale when steps are newer than evidence.
Delete runner_state.json every time the WORKFLOW page renders.

regression_tests.description: Verify completed steps are reflected from run logs.
Verify failed step and blocked downstream status are reflected from run logs.
Verify lab_stages.toml newer than log marks preview stale.

student_answer.diagnosis: The stale view is not primarily a Graphviz rendering cache issue.
student_answer.root_cause: The preview reuses previous execution evidence without comparing the project-step digest or checking whether lab_stages.toml is newer than the latest run log.

cases.title: Audit a 5e diagnostic exercise against the 2026 cycle 4 program
cases.student_prompt: Check whether a 5e self-evaluation sheet covers the top-level 2026 cycle 4 mathematics domains.
cases.symptom: A 5e diagnostic sheet mixes calculations and geometry but does not mention data handling, proportionality, functions, or computational thinking.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The sheet is complete because it includes arithmetic and a geometric construction.
cases.root_cause: The diagnosis is incomplete because the 2026 5e rollout requires coverage across numbers and algebra, space and geometry, data and probability, proportionality and functions, and computational thinking.
cases.plain_repro: Compare the exercise metadata curriculum_ids with the 2026 cycle 4 required_program_ids for level 5e.
weak_assumptions: Arithmetic and geometry are enough to represent the 5e program.
A self-evaluation sheet can ignore data, proportionality, functions, and computational thinking.

evidence.description: The coverage contract lists five required top-level 5e curriculum ids.
The checked sheet lacks curriculum ids for data, proportionality, functions, and computational thinking.

candidate_fixes.summary: Tag or add exercises so all five 5e top-level curriculum ids are covered.
Keep the sheet unchanged and rename it as complete.

regression_tests.description: Assert every 2026 5e required curriculum id meets the minimum exercise count.
Reject cases that reference unknown curriculum ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: The sheet is not complete just because it includes arithmetic and geometry.
student_answer.root_cause: The 2026 5e rollout requires coverage across numbers and algebra, space and geometry, data and probability, proportionality and functions, and computational thinking.

cases.title: Audit a seconde GT sheet against the 2026 program
cases.student_prompt: Check whether a seconde GT exercise bundle covers the 2026 top-level domains.
cases.symptom: A seconde bundle contains algebra and functions but no explicit logic, algorithmic, geometry, automatisms, or statistics/probability coverage.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The bundle covers seconde because algebra and functions dominate the year.
cases.root_cause: The diagnosis is incomplete because the 2026 seconde GT program also requires logic and sets, algorithmic programming, automatisms, geometry, and statistics and probabilities.
cases.plain_repro: Run the TeSciA math_program_2026 coverage report and inspect missing seconde_gt curriculum ids.
weak_assumptions: Algebra and functions alone are representative enough.
Algorithmic and statistics domains are optional extension topics.

evidence.description: The coverage contract lists seven seconde GT top-level curriculum ids.
The bundle metadata does not include logic, algorithmic, geometry, automatisms, or statistics/probability ids.

candidate_fixes.summary: Add or tag exercises for all seven seconde GT top-level curriculum ids.
Keep the bundle focused only on algebra and functions.

regression_tests.description: Assert every 2026 seconde GT curriculum id is covered.
Assert the generated coverage report has no missing seconde_gt ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: The bundle is incomplete if it only checks algebra and functions.
student_answer.root_cause: The 2026 seconde GT program also requires logic and sets, algorithmic programming, automatisms, geometry, and statistics and probabilities.

cases.title: Audit premiere general mathematics integrated in scientific education
cases.student_prompt: Check whether a premiere general self-evaluation bundle covers the mathematics integrated in scientific education.
cases.symptom: A premiere general bundle checks percentages but omits random phenomena and evolution models.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The bundle is complete because it covers numerical information.
cases.root_cause: The diagnosis is incomplete because the 2026 integrated mathematics path also requires automatisms, random phenomena, and evolution phenomena modeled by functions.
cases.plain_repro: Filter required_program_ids where track is lycee_generale_enseignement_scientifique and compare them with case curriculum_ids.
weak_assumptions: Information chiffree is the only required integrated mathematics theme.
Random phenomena and evolution models are covered elsewhere automatically.

evidence.description: The coverage contract lists four top-level integrated mathematics ids for premiere generale.
The bundle omits random phenomena and evolution-function modeling ids.

candidate_fixes.summary: Add exercises for automatisms, numerical information, random phenomena, and evolution models.
Keep only numerical information exercises.

regression_tests.description: Assert every integrated premiere general required curriculum id is covered.
Assert the coverage report records the official source URL for the track.

student_answer.diagnosis: The bundle cannot stop at numerical information.
student_answer.root_cause: The 2026 integrated mathematics path also requires automatisms, random phenomena, and evolution phenomena modeled by functions.

cases.title: Audit premiere generale specialite mathematics coverage
cases.student_prompt: Check whether a premiere specialite self-evaluation catalog covers the 2026 top-level domains.
cases.symptom: A premiere specialite catalog has strong analysis exercises but lacks algebra, geometry, probability/statistics, experimentation, and algorithmic coverage.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The catalog is complete because analysis is the central domain.
cases.root_cause: The diagnosis is incomplete because the 2026 premiere specialite program requires logic and algorithmic work, automatisms, algebra, analysis, geometry, probabilities and statistics, and experimentations.
cases.plain_repro: Compare premiere_generale_specialite required_program_ids against the catalog curriculum_ids.
weak_assumptions: Analysis coverage can stand in for every specialite domain.
Experimentations and algorithmic work do not need explicit self-evaluation items.

evidence.description: The coverage contract lists seven premiere specialite top-level curriculum ids.
The catalog lacks explicit tags for several non-analysis domains.

candidate_fixes.summary: Add or tag exercises for all seven premiere specialite top-level domains.
Keep only the analysis-heavy catalog and document the rest as future work.

regression_tests.description: Assert every premiere specialite required curriculum id is covered.
Reject unknown premiere specialite curriculum ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: Analysis coverage alone does not make the specialite catalog complete.
student_answer.root_cause: The 2026 premiere specialite program requires logic and algorithmic work, automatisms, algebra, analysis, geometry, probabilities and statistics, and experimentations.

cases.title: Audit premiere technologique mathematics coverage
cases.student_prompt: Check whether a premiere technologique self-evaluation catalog covers the 2026 top-level domains.
cases.symptom: A premiere technologique catalog emphasizes applied functions but has no explicit statistics/probability, geometry, logic, algorithmic, or automatisms coverage.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The catalog is complete because applied functions cover the technological needs.
cases.root_cause: The diagnosis is incomplete because the 2026 premiere technologique program requires logic and algorithmic work, geometric activities, automatisms, analysis, and statistics and probabilities.
cases.plain_repro: Compare premiere_technologique required_program_ids against the catalog curriculum_ids.
weak_assumptions: Applied functions are enough for the technological pathway.
Statistics, geometry, and algorithmic work can remain implicit.

evidence.description: The coverage contract lists five premiere technologique top-level curriculum ids.
The catalog lacks explicit ids for several required domains.

candidate_fixes.summary: Add or tag exercises for all five premiere technologique top-level domains.
Keep only applied-function exercises.

regression_tests.description: Assert every premiere technologique required curriculum id is covered.
Assert the generated coverage report has no missing premiere technologique ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: Applied functions alone are not enough for the premiere technologique catalog.
student_answer.root_cause: The 2026 premiere technologique program requires logic and algorithmic work, geometric activities, automatisms, analysis, and statistics and probabilities.

cases.title: Second-pass 5e coverage practice
cases.student_prompt: Audit a second 5e practice round and verify that every 2026 top-level domain remains visible.
cases.symptom: A second 5e practice round has been added but its metadata may duplicate only one domain.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: A second round is useful even if it repeats only the first arithmetic skill.
cases.root_cause: The second practice round must reinforce every 2026 5e top-level domain so the catalog has more than one exercise per curriculum id.
cases.plain_repro: Check curriculum_id_counts for every cycle4_5e id.
weak_assumptions: One exercise per curriculum id is enough for self-evaluation quality.
A second round can be counted even when it covers only arithmetic.

evidence.description: The coverage contract requires at least two cases per curriculum id.
The second 5e practice round declares all five cycle 4 ids.

candidate_fixes.summary: Keep the second round mapped to all five 5e domains.
Keep the second round mapped only to arithmetic.

regression_tests.description: Assert all 5e curriculum ids have at least two cases.
Assert undercovered_curriculum_ids excludes cycle4 ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: The second 5e round must not repeat only arithmetic.
student_answer.root_cause: The second practice round must reinforce every 2026 5e top-level domain so the catalog has more than one exercise per curriculum id.

cases.title: Second-pass seconde GT coverage practice
cases.student_prompt: Audit a second seconde GT exercise round and verify coverage-count quality.
cases.symptom: The seconde catalog has one complete audit case, but quality checks require a second practice round.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The first complete seconde case is enough.
cases.root_cause: The 2026 seconde GT catalog needs a second case covering logic and sets, algorithmic programming, automatisms, numbers and algebra, geometry, functions, and statistics and probabilities.
cases.plain_repro: Check curriculum_id_counts for every seconde_gt id.
weak_assumptions: Completeness and quality thresholds are the same.
A single diagnostic case is enough practice for each seconde domain.

evidence.description: The contract requires at least two cases per seconde curriculum id.
The second seconde round declares all seven seconde ids.

candidate_fixes.summary: Keep the second seconde round mapped to every required id.
Keep only the first seconde audit case.

regression_tests.description: Assert all seconde curriculum ids have at least two cases.
Assert undercovered_curriculum_ids excludes seconde ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: A single complete seconde case is not enough for the quality threshold.
student_answer.root_cause: The 2026 seconde GT catalog needs a second case covering logic and sets, algorithmic programming, automatisms, numbers and algebra, geometry, functions, and statistics and probabilities.

cases.title: Second-pass premiere ES integrated mathematics practice
cases.student_prompt: Audit a second integrated mathematics round for premiere generale.
cases.symptom: The integrated mathematics path has one complete case but not enough repeated practice.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: Integrated mathematics can rely on one diagnostic case.
cases.root_cause: The integrated mathematics path needs a second case covering automatisms, numerical information, random phenomena, and evolution models.
cases.plain_repro: Check curriculum_id_counts for every premiere_generale_es id.
weak_assumptions: One integrated case is sufficient for repeated self-evaluation.
Evolution and random phenomena do not need second-pass checks.

evidence.description: The contract requires at least two cases per integrated mathematics id.
The second integrated mathematics round declares all four ids.

candidate_fixes.summary: Keep a second integrated mathematics round covering all required ids.
Keep only one integrated mathematics round.

regression_tests.description: Assert all integrated mathematics ids have at least two cases.
Assert undercovered_curriculum_ids excludes integrated mathematics ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: The integrated path needs repeated practice, not one complete case only.
student_answer.root_cause: The integrated mathematics path needs a second case covering automatisms, numerical information, random phenomena, and evolution models.

cases.title: Second-pass premiere specialite mathematics practice
cases.student_prompt: Audit a second premiere specialite exercise round for repeated domain coverage.
cases.symptom: The premiere specialite catalog has one broad case but no second-pass practice across all domains.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: A single broad specialite case is enough because it is advanced.
cases.root_cause: The premiere specialite catalog needs a second case covering logic and algorithmic work, automatisms, algebra, analysis, geometry, probabilities and statistics, and experimentations.
cases.plain_repro: Check curriculum_id_counts for every premiere_generale_specialite id.
weak_assumptions: Advanced students do not need repeated domain practice.
Experimentations and algorithmic work can be represented by one broad case.

evidence.description: The contract requires at least two cases per specialite id.
The second specialite round declares all seven ids.

candidate_fixes.summary: Keep a second specialite round covering all required ids.
Keep only one specialite coverage case.

regression_tests.description: Assert all premiere specialite ids have at least two cases.
Assert undercovered_curriculum_ids excludes specialite ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: The specialite catalog needs second-pass practice across all domains.
student_answer.root_cause: The premiere specialite catalog needs a second case covering logic and algorithmic work, automatisms, algebra, analysis, geometry, probabilities and statistics, and experimentations.

cases.title: Second-pass premiere technologique mathematics practice
cases.student_prompt: Audit a second premiere technologique exercise round for repeated coverage.
cases.symptom: The premiere technologique catalog has one broad coverage case but no second round.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: A second round is unnecessary if the first case covers every domain.
cases.root_cause: The premiere technologique catalog needs a second case covering logic and algorithmic work, geometric activities, automatisms, analysis, and statistics and probabilities.
cases.plain_repro: Check curriculum_id_counts for every premiere_technologique id.
weak_assumptions: One broad technological case is enough for every student.
Statistics and algorithmic work do not need repeated self-evaluation.

evidence.description: The contract requires at least two cases per technological id.
The second technological round declares all five ids.

candidate_fixes.summary: Keep a second technological round covering all required ids.
Keep only one technological coverage case.

regression_tests.description: Assert all premiere technologique ids have at least two cases.
Assert undercovered_curriculum_ids excludes technological ids.

student_answer.diagnosis: The technological catalog still needs a second practice round.
student_answer.root_cause: The premiere technologique catalog needs a second case covering logic and algorithmic work, geometric activities, automatisms, analysis, and statistics and probabilities.

cases.title: Modernize a legacy Python and pandas interview answer
cases.student_prompt: Review a legacy data-scientist QCM answer key. Identify the outdated Python and pandas claims, select the evidence-backed correction, and define regression checks for the refreshed interview bank.
cases.symptom: A candidate answer key still says venv is for sharing one environment across projects, pyenv is for one virtual environment per project, DataFrame.append is acceptable, and ordinary equality joins are enough for timestamp-aligned sensor data.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The answer key only needs spelling cleanup because the Python fundamentals are still correct.
cases.root_cause: The legacy key mixes durable Python fundamentals with outdated tooling guidance; a 2026 data-scientist evaluation should require isolated virtual environments, separate Python-version management, pandas.concat instead of removed DataFrame.append, and merge_asof for nearest-key time joins on sorted data.
cases.plain_repro: Run the legacy pandas append snippet under pandas 2+ and review a time-series join where samples and labels have nearest timestamps instead of identical timestamps.
weak_assumptions: Old pandas idioms remain acceptable if they were once common.
Environment isolation and Python-version selection are the same concept.
All tabular joins are equality joins even when the key is time.

evidence.description: pandas 2 removed Series.append and DataFrame.append; concat is the maintained replacement.
Timestamp-aligned telemetry and label data often need nearest-key joins with sorted inputs rather than exact equality joins.
venv isolates project dependencies while pyenv-style tooling selects Python interpreter versions.
Notebook helper functions that mutate module globals are not safe when reused in parallel scoring or worker execution.

candidate_fixes.summary: Refresh the answer key with current Python environment boundaries, pandas 2+ APIs, explicit time-join guidance, and reentrant helper expectations.
Keep the legacy answers and add a generic warning that some library APIs may have changed.

regression_tests.description: Assert the refreshed bank no longer recommends DataFrame.append and names pandas.concat.
Assert timestamp-join prompts mention merge_asof or an equivalent nearest-key sorted join.
Assert the environment section separates virtual environments from Python-version management.

student_answer.diagnosis: The old QCM has valid Python fundamentals but the tooling guidance is now stale.
student_answer.root_cause: The legacy key mixes durable Python fundamentals with outdated tooling guidance; a 2026 data-scientist evaluation should require isolated virtual environments, separate Python-version management, pandas.concat instead of removed DataFrame.append, and merge_asof for nearest-key time joins on sorted data.

cases.title: Evaluate an imbalanced model without leakage
cases.student_prompt: A fraud classifier reports 98 percent accuracy on a two percent positive class. Diagnose the weak evaluation, pick the safer fix, and choose regression checks.
cases.symptom: The notebook fits imputation, scaling, PCA, and synthetic oversampling before the train-test split, then selects the model by accuracy on a heavily imbalanced target.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The score is high enough because accuracy is intuitive and easy to explain.
cases.root_cause: The evaluation leaks test-set information through preprocessing and resampling, and accuracy hides minority-class failure; the pipeline should fit every learned transform inside cross-validation and report task-aligned metrics such as precision, recall, F1, PR-AUC, ROC-AUC, calibration, and threshold tradeoffs.
cases.plain_repro: Run the notebook with a Pipeline that fits preprocessing only on training folds, compare accuracy with PR-AUC and recall at the required precision floor, and inspect whether test data is ever passed to fit.
weak_assumptions: A high accuracy proves a classifier is useful on rare positives.
Preprocessing is harmless if labels are not passed explicitly.
The model threshold can be chosen after looking at test performance.

evidence.description: The scaler, PCA, and sampler are fit before train-test separation.
Only two percent of rows are positive, so a naive negative classifier can look accurate.
False negatives and false positives have different operational costs and require an explicit threshold policy.

candidate_fixes.summary: Move learned preprocessing and resampling into a Pipeline, use stratified cross-validation, and gate the model on minority-class and calibrated-threshold metrics.
Keep the preprocessing order and optimize accuracy because it is easy to communicate.

regression_tests.description: Static or notebook-level check that StandardScaler, imputers, PCA, and samplers are fit only inside training folds.
Assert the model report includes PR-AUC or average precision, recall, precision, F1, ROC-AUC, and a confusion matrix.
Replay the selected threshold on a holdout set and record the precision-recall tradeoff.

student_answer.diagnosis: The high accuracy is misleading because the workflow leaks information and ignores the rare positive class.
student_answer.root_cause: The evaluation leaks test-set information through preprocessing and resampling, and accuracy hides minority-class failure; the pipeline should fit every learned transform inside cross-validation and report task-aligned metrics such as precision, recall, F1, PR-AUC, ROC-AUC, calibration, and threshold tradeoffs.

cases.title: Choose the right scaling path for a feature pipeline
cases.student_prompt: A feature pipeline is slow and close to memory limits. Decide whether to optimize locally, use Dask, use Ray Data, or move to a heavier cluster path.
cases.symptom: A candidate proposes Spark immediately for a 60 GB CSV feature job without profiling, converting to Parquet, choosing partition sizes, or checking vectorized pandas and NumPy alternatives.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: Any dataset larger than memory should immediately move to a cluster framework.
cases.root_cause: The scaling diagnosis skips cheaper discriminators; the current pipeline needs profiling, columnar storage, partition and memory sizing, vectorized or compiled local baselines, then Dask for pandas-like out-of-core tabular work or Ray Data when the workload is an AI batch-inference or training ingest pipeline.
cases.plain_repro: Profile peak memory and wall time on one representative partition, convert the CSV sample to Parquet, and compare local vectorized pandas, Dask DataFrame, and Ray Data batch execution on the same feature contract.
weak_assumptions: Big data is defined only by raw file size.
A cluster framework fixes inefficient algorithms and poor file formats.
Dask, Ray Data, and Spark have the same best use case.

evidence.description: No local baseline, memory profile, or algorithmic hotspot report is attached.
Most time is spent scanning wide CSV input that can be converted to columnar storage.
The downstream stage performs GPU batch inference and needs streaming batches to keep accelerators busy.

candidate_fixes.summary: Create a measured baseline, convert to efficient columnar storage, size partitions, and choose Dask or Ray Data only when the measured workload matches their strengths.
Start by rewriting the pipeline in a cluster framework before profiling or changing storage format.

regression_tests.description: Require memory, I/O, and wall-time baseline artifacts before engine migration is accepted.
Verify the feature contract on a partitioned Parquet sample before distributed execution.
Require a decision row that states why local pandas, Dask, Ray Data, or Spark was selected.

student_answer.diagnosis: The framework choice is premature because the workload has not been profiled.
student_answer.root_cause: The scaling diagnosis skips cheaper discriminators; the current pipeline needs profiling, columnar storage, partition and memory sizing, vectorized or compiled local baselines, then Dask for pandas-like out-of-core tabular work or Ray Data when the workload is an AI batch-inference or training ingest pipeline.

cases.title: Evaluate a RAG assistant with evidence and risk gates
cases.student_prompt: A team wants to ship a RAG assistant after a good manual demo. Build the evidence-backed evaluation and risk plan expected from a current data-scientist interview.
cases.symptom: The proposed chatbot over internal documents has one happy-path demo, no retrieval goldens, no citation checks, no privacy tests, and no monitoring plan for groundedness or drift.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The demo is convincing enough because the underlying foundation model is strong.
cases.root_cause: A 2026 data-scientist evaluation must treat GenAI as an evidence and risk-management workflow: retrieval quality, answer groundedness, citation coverage, refusal behavior, privacy redaction, prompt-injection resistance, and post-deployment drift need explicit tests before launch.
cases.plain_repro: Run a fixed RAG golden set with expected document ids, adversarial prompts, private-data probes, and stale-document cases; compare retrieved ids, cited spans, refusal decisions, and answer-groundedness scores.
weak_assumptions: A strong base model removes the need for task-specific evaluation.
One demo can represent retrieval quality and safety behavior.
Privacy and prompt-injection failures are outside the data-scientist evaluation scope.

evidence.description: There is no dataset of questions mapped to expected documents or answer spans.
The report does not verify whether generated claims are supported by retrieved context.
No tests cover private-data leakage, prompt injection, or unsafe refusal boundaries.
Current AI risk guidance treats generative AI risks as requiring explicit governance actions.

candidate_fixes.summary: Build a RAG evaluation set with retrieval, groundedness, citation, privacy, injection, refusal, and drift gates before release.
Ship the assistant after a manual demo and rely on users to report poor answers.

regression_tests.description: Assert expected document ids appear in top-k retrieval for the golden set.
Assert answer claims cite retrieved spans and unsupported claims are rejected.
Run private-data probes and prompt-injection cases against the assistant.
Record retrieval corpus version, model version, and live failure-rate drift thresholds.

student_answer.diagnosis: The demo is not enough because RAG quality and GenAI risk behavior are untested.
student_answer.root_cause: A 2026 data-scientist evaluation must treat GenAI as an evidence and risk-management workflow: retrieval quality, answer groundedness, citation coverage, refusal behavior, privacy redaction, prompt-injection resistance, and post-deployment drift need explicit tests before launch.

cases.title: Compress and deploy a neural model without losing the evidence trail
cases.student_prompt: A candidate suggests reducing neural-network size by changing float tensors to integers. Diagnose what is missing from the deployment-quality answer.
cases.symptom: The answer key lists dropout, pruning, quantification, and casting floats to integers as interchangeable ways to reduce inference cost, with no accuracy, latency, hardware, or calibration-data gates.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The answer is acceptable because it names common compression techniques.
cases.root_cause: The answer confuses training regularization with deployment compression; a current answer should distinguish pruning, quantization-aware or post-training quantization, distillation, mixed precision, compilation, and runtime export, then prove accuracy and latency on the target hardware with representative calibration and holdout data.
cases.plain_repro: Benchmark the baseline model and each compressed candidate on the target runtime, recording model size, latency percentiles, throughput, memory, and accuracy deltas on calibration and holdout sets.
weak_assumptions: Casting tensors to integers is equivalent to validated quantization.
Dropout is an inference-time compression method.
Model size reduction is safe without a target-runtime accuracy and latency gate.

evidence.description: The answer key groups training regularization and deployment optimization as one technique family.
No CPU, GPU, or accelerator-specific latency and memory measurement is attached.
Quantization is proposed without representative calibration data or an accepted accuracy-delta threshold.

candidate_fixes.summary: Separate compression techniques, select the runtime path, and require calibration, holdout accuracy, latency, throughput, memory, and rollback gates.
Cast floating-point weights to integers and accept the smaller artifact if it loads.

regression_tests.description: Assert every compressed artifact meets an agreed accuracy delta and latency percentile gate.
Assert the quantization report records calibration dataset identity and hash.
Smoke-test the exported artifact on the declared CPU or GPU runtime before accepting it.

student_answer.diagnosis: The answer names real techniques but mixes them and lacks deployment evidence.
student_answer.root_cause: The answer confuses training regularization with deployment compression; a current answer should distinguish pruning, quantization-aware or post-training quantization, distillation, mixed precision, compilation, and runtime export, then prove accuracy and latency on the target hardware with representative calibration and holdout data.

cases.title: Design the retrieval layer before adding agents
cases.student_prompt: A support assistant returns plausible but poorly grounded answers. Decide whether to move to Graph RAG, agentic RAG, or a measured retrieval-layer redesign.
cases.symptom: The current system chunks PDFs by fixed character count, retrieves top-4 dense vectors with no metadata filters, no reranker, no permission boundary, and no golden set, then the candidate proposes adding an agent planner as the first fix.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The RAG pipeline is too simple; moving to agentic Graph RAG should solve the retrieval failures.
cases.root_cause: The weak point is the unmeasured retrieval layer, not the absence of agents; a current answer should evaluate chunking, metadata filters, hybrid or vector retrieval, reranking, context assembly, permission checks, and top-k recall before escalating to Graph RAG or agentic RAG for genuine multi-hop, relationship-heavy, or tool-use workflows.
cases.plain_repro: Run a retrieval golden set with expected document ids and answer spans, compare fixed chunks versus structure-aware chunks, test metadata filters, add a reranker, and measure recall, precision, latency, and context-token budget before adding an agent loop.
weak_assumptions: Agentic RAG fixes poor retrieval evidence automatically.
Graph RAG is always better than dense retrieval for enterprise documents.
Top-k vector similarity is enough without filters, reranking, or permission checks.

evidence.description: There is no question set mapped to expected documents, spans, or negative cases.
Fixed-size chunks were chosen without comparing structure-aware chunks, overlap, or parent-document context.
The retrieval path ignores metadata filters, access-control constraints, and candidate reranking.
Retrieved context is not tracked against token budget, latency, or answer quality.

candidate_fixes.summary: Instrument the retrieval layer with goldens, chunking variants, filters, reranking, access checks, context budgeting, and escalation criteria for graph or agentic routing.
Replace the current pipeline with an agentic Graph RAG stack before measuring the retrieval failure mode.

regression_tests.description: Assert expected documents and spans appear in top-k results across a versioned golden set.
Verify metadata filters, permission checks, reranking, and context-token budgets are recorded for each retrieval run.
Require a decision record that states when graph traversal or agent planning is justified.

student_answer.diagnosis: The proposed agent layer is premature because retrieval quality has not been measured.
student_answer.root_cause: The weak point is the unmeasured retrieval layer, not the absence of agents; a current answer should evaluate chunking, metadata filters, hybrid or vector retrieval, reranking, context assembly, permission checks, and top-k recall before escalating to Graph RAG or agentic RAG for genuine multi-hop, relationship-heavy, or tool-use workflows.

cases.title: Make agent memory useful instead of noisy
cases.student_prompt: An AI agent keeps forgetting project decisions and sometimes leaks obsolete context. Design the evidence-backed memory fix.
cases.symptom: The proposed implementation embeds every chat message into a single vector collection, has no memory schema, no namespace per project, no retention policy, no source provenance, and no recall or stale-memory evaluation.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The agent needs more memory; storing every message in vectors is the safest default.
cases.root_cause: Agent memory quality is governed by schema, provenance, retention, retrieval policy, and evaluation; a production-grade answer should separate episodic, semantic, procedural, and user preference memories, version the schema, namespace by project, redact private data, expire stale entries, and measure memory precision, recall, and harm.
cases.plain_repro: Replay tasks that require a previous decision, a stale decision that must be ignored, and a private value that must not be recalled; compare raw transcript embedding against a schema-versioned memory store with retrieval and retention gates.
weak_assumptions: More remembered text always improves an agent.
A vector database is a complete memory architecture.
Privacy, expiry, and provenance can be handled after the agent is useful.

evidence.description: All messages are embedded without typed memory records or source references.
Project boundaries, user boundaries, expiry, and stale-memory invalidation are missing.
No test prevents sensitive values from being stored or recalled in future prompts.
There are no tasks measuring useful recall, harmful recall, or stale-memory suppression.

candidate_fixes.summary: Create typed, versioned, namespaced memory records with provenance, retention, privacy redaction, and replay tests for useful and harmful recall.
Embed all messages in one collection and rely on semantic search to find the useful memories.

regression_tests.description: Reject memory records without type, namespace, source, created-at, expiry, and redaction status.
Replay tasks that require a correct memory and tasks that must suppress stale or private memory.
Export memory precision, recall, stale-hit rate, and privacy-hit rate for a fixed evaluation set.

student_answer.diagnosis: The agent does not need unbounded memory; it needs a governed memory contract.
student_answer.root_cause: Agent memory quality is governed by schema, provenance, retention, retrieval policy, and evaluation; a production-grade answer should separate episodic, semantic, procedural, and user preference memories, version the schema, namespace by project, redact private data, expire stale entries, and measure memory precision, recall, and harm.

cases.title: Evaluate generated answers beyond a leaderboard score
cases.student_prompt: A candidate selects a model because it leads a public benchmark. Build the evaluation plan for a domain assistant that writes analyst summaries.
cases.symptom: The answer cites one leaderboard, has no domain golden set, no verifier checks for structured claims, no calibrated LLM judge, no human spot-check policy, and no regression taxonomy.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The best public benchmark model should be chosen because benchmarks summarize model quality objectively.
cases.root_cause: Public benchmarks are useful but insufficient for a task-specific generative system; a current answer should combine task goldens, multiple-choice or benchmark context, deterministic verifiers, calibrated LLM judges, human review on ambiguous cases, confidence intervals, and a failure taxonomy that feeds regression tests.
cases.plain_repro: Run the candidate models on a versioned analyst-summary golden set, verify required facts and forbidden claims with deterministic checks, calibrate judge agreement against human labels, and report confidence intervals and failure categories.
weak_assumptions: A public leaderboard is a deployment evaluation.
An LLM judge is objective without calibration against human labels.
Generated text can be accepted without deterministic checks for required facts.

evidence.description: Model choice is justified by one public benchmark with no local task evidence.
There is no versioned set of representative summaries, edge cases, or expected factual constraints.
The proposed judge score is not compared with human labels or deterministic verifier outcomes.
The report lacks confidence intervals, error categories, and regression-test routing.

candidate_fixes.summary: Build a layered evaluation with task goldens, deterministic verifiers, calibrated LLM judges, human review policy, confidence intervals, and a regression taxonomy.
Use the current leaderboard winner and monitor user feedback after launch.

regression_tests.description: Fail model promotion when a versioned task-specific golden set is missing.
Compare deterministic verifier results and judge ratings against sampled human labels.
Export hallucination, omission, formatting, safety, and retrieval failure classes for regression tracking.

student_answer.diagnosis: Leaderboard performance is only context; it does not prove domain assistant quality.
student_answer.root_cause: Public benchmarks are useful but insufficient for a task-specific generative system; a current answer should combine task goldens, multiple-choice or benchmark context, deterministic verifiers, calibrated LLM judges, human review on ambiguous cases, confidence intervals, and a failure taxonomy that feeds regression tests.

cases.title: Handle uncertainty and drift in production predictions
cases.student_prompt: A demand-forecasting model looks strong in cross-validation but fails on a new region. Diagnose the uncertainty and distribution-shift gap.
cases.symptom: The candidate reports mean cross-validation error and a bootstrap confidence interval, then promises the same reliability in a new region without a calibration set, conformal coverage check, covariate-shift monitor, or retraining policy.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The model is reliable because the average validation score and confidence interval are acceptable.
cases.root_cause: The answer confuses average predictive performance with calibrated uncertainty under deployment shift; a current plan should keep a representative calibration set, report conformal prediction coverage where exchangeability is plausible, monitor covariate and label drift, compare train-test discordance, and define recalibration or retraining triggers.
cases.plain_repro: Split data by time and region, compute conformal interval coverage on a held-out calibration and deployment-like slice, then track feature drift, label drift when labels arrive, and coverage degradation after rollout.
weak_assumptions: A confidence interval on average error gives each prediction a valid uncertainty set.
Cross-validation performance transfers unchanged to a new region.
Drift monitoring can wait until users complain.

evidence.description: The deployment region has different feature distributions and seasonality from the training regions.
Prediction intervals are reported without empirical coverage on a held-out calibration or deployment-like slice.
The report hides regional failure behind an aggregate mean error.
There is no policy for recalibration, retraining, or rollback when drift and coverage degrade.

candidate_fixes.summary: Use deployment-like validation, conformal coverage checks, segment-level metrics, drift monitors, and explicit recalibration or retraining triggers.
Ship with the cross-validation score and a confidence interval on the mean validation error.

regression_tests.description: Assert interval coverage and error are reported by region, time slice, and important segments.
Simulate feature and label drift and assert alert thresholds are recorded.
Replay a degraded-coverage period and verify the recalibration or retraining trigger fires.

student_answer.diagnosis: The validation summary does not prove prediction reliability under regional shift.
student_answer.root_cause: The answer confuses average predictive performance with calibrated uncertainty under deployment shift; a current plan should keep a representative calibration set, report conformal prediction coverage where exchangeability is plausible, monitor covariate and label drift, compare train-test discordance, and define recalibration or retraining triggers.

cases.title: Reduce LLM serving cost with measured quality gates
cases.student_prompt: An LLM summarization workflow is too expensive. Choose the stronger cost-saving plan without degrading task quality.
cases.symptom: The candidate recommends switching to the smallest model immediately. The trace shows repeated static instructions, duplicated retrieved context, no prompt-cache accounting, no batching or paged-attention consideration, no speculative-decoding compatibility check, and no quality regression set.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The only reliable way to reduce LLM cost is to use a smaller model.
cases.root_cause: LLM cost needs a measured stack, not a single model swap; a current answer should baseline tokens, latency, cache hit rate, and quality, then test static-prefix prompt caching, retrieval/context pruning, batching or paged attention, quantization or distillation, and speculative decoding when the serving stack supports it, with task-quality and safety gates.
cases.plain_repro: Replay the summarization golden set while recording input tokens, output tokens, cacheable prefix length, cache hit rate, latency percentiles, throughput, and quality before and after prompt caching, context pruning, batching, and decoding changes.
weak_assumptions: A smaller model is always cheaper after quality retries and longer prompts.
Prompt tokens are fixed cost even when a static prefix can be cached.
Serving optimizations can be accepted without a task-quality regression suite.

evidence.description: The workflow has no baseline for tokens, latency, cacheability, retries, or output quality.
A long system and instruction prefix is repeated across requests and could be cacheable.
Retrieved chunks contain duplicate sections that consume context without adding evidence.
The answer does not check whether batching, paged attention, quantization, distillation, or speculative decoding are supported by the selected runtime.

candidate_fixes.summary: Baseline cost and quality, then test prompt caching, context pruning, batching, runtime optimization, and speculative decoding only behind quality and safety gates.
Switch to the smallest available model and accept any quality change if the bill is lower.

regression_tests.description: Export per-run token counts, cache hit rate, latency, retries, cost estimate, and quality metrics.
Replay a golden set with context deduplication and pruning, asserting citations and quality remain stable.
Fail optimization claims when runtime support for caching, batching, paged attention, quantization, or speculative decoding is not recorded.

student_answer.diagnosis: The cost plan skips measurement and may trade away quality unnecessarily.
student_answer.root_cause: LLM cost needs a measured stack, not a single model swap; a current answer should baseline tokens, latency, cache hit rate, and quality, then test static-prefix prompt caching, retrieval/context pruning, batching or paged attention, quantization or distillation, and speculative decoding when the serving stack supports it, with task-quality and safety gates.

cases.title: Improve a limited-label model with data-centric evidence
cases.student_prompt: A vision classifier has few labels and unstable validation scores. Decide whether to collect data, change labels, use self-supervision, or train a larger model.
cases.symptom: The candidate proposes a larger transformer and more epochs, but the dataset has duplicate images, label disagreement, underrepresented classes, no confidence intervals on validation scores, and no active-learning or self-supervised baseline.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The model underfits because it is not large enough for modern computer vision.
cases.root_cause: Limited-label performance is often data and evidence limited before it is model-size limited; a current answer should audit label quality and duplicates, inspect segment errors, report uncertainty, compare augmentation, active learning, weak or self-supervised pretraining, and only then justify a larger architecture.
cases.plain_repro: Run duplicate detection, label-agreement audit, segment-level error analysis, confidence intervals across resampling folds, and compare a small transfer-learning baseline with augmentation, active-learning selection, and self-supervised pretraining.
weak_assumptions: A larger model is the default answer when labeled data is scarce.
Validation accuracy is stable enough without uncertainty estimates.
Label quality and class coverage are separate from model evaluation.

evidence.description: Manual review shows conflicting labels for visually similar or duplicate images.
Two deployment-relevant classes have too few labeled examples to estimate performance reliably.
The report gives one validation score with no confidence interval or fold-level variance.
No augmentation, active-learning, weak-supervision, or self-supervised baseline is compared.

candidate_fixes.summary: Audit label quality, duplicates, segment coverage, and uncertainty, then compare augmentation, active learning, weak supervision, self-supervised pretraining, and architecture changes.
Train a larger transformer for more epochs before spending time on label and data-quality checks.

regression_tests.description: Fail the case when duplicate detection, label agreement, and class-coverage artifacts are missing.
Require confidence intervals or fold-level variance for limited-label validation scores.
Compare baseline, augmentation, active-learning selection, and self-supervised or transfer-learning paths before accepting a bigger model.

student_answer.diagnosis: The evidence points to label and coverage limits before architecture limits.
student_answer.root_cause: Limited-label performance is often data and evidence limited before it is model-size limited; a current answer should audit label quality and duplicates, inspect segment errors, report uncertainty, compare augmentation, active learning, weak or self-supervised pretraining, and only then justify a larger architecture.

cases.title: Review an open-weight LLM release like an engineer
cases.student_prompt: A team wants to replace its current model after reading an impressive open-weight LLM release note. Decide what a serious review must check before migration.
cases.symptom: The candidate compares only headline benchmark scores and context-window length. The release note also changes attention design, KV-cache behavior, tokenizer, license terms, reasoning mode, quantized checkpoints, and serving memory requirements.
cases.proposed_diagnosis: The new model should be adopted because it has a larger context window and better public benchmark scores.
cases.root_cause: A serious open-weight LLM review must connect architecture details to workload evidence; the answer should inspect attention and KV-cache tradeoffs, tokenizer and prompt compatibility, license and data-use constraints, quantization support, reasoning-mode latency, domain eval results, safety behavior, and target serving memory before migration.
cases.plain_repro: Run the current and candidate models on the same domain prompts, long-context retrieval cases, safety probes, and latency-memory benchmarks, then compare tokenizer effects, KV-cache memory, output quality, cost, and license constraints.
weak_assumptions: A larger context window guarantees better long-context answers.
Attention architecture changes do not affect serving memory and latency.
Public benchmark gains transfer automatically to the target workload.

evidence.description: The decision ignores attention, KV-cache, tokenizer, reasoning-mode, and quantization changes.
No local benchmark covers the team's prompts, retrieval cases, or safety constraints.
The candidate model has not been profiled for target hardware memory, latency, and throughput.
The migration plan does not record whether license and data-use terms fit the product context.

candidate_fixes.summary: Gate model migration on architecture delta review, workload-specific evals, safety probes, license review, quantization support, and serving memory-latency benchmarks.
Adopt the model with the strongest public benchmark and largest context window.

regression_tests.description: Run a fixed domain and long-context prompt suite before and after the model swap.
Record memory, throughput, and latency under representative context lengths and batch sizes.
Require recorded license compatibility and safety-probe results before promotion.

student_answer.diagnosis: The migration decision lacks workload evidence and architecture-risk review.
student_answer.root_cause: A serious open-weight LLM review must connect architecture details to workload evidence; the answer should inspect attention and KV-cache tradeoffs, tokenizer and prompt compatibility, license and data-use constraints, quantization support, reasoning-mode latency, domain eval results, safety behavior, and target serving memory before migration.
